audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Deuteronomy / Devarim festival, judge, and anti-cult-object law, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Passover, Unleavened Bread, Weeks, Booths, chosen-place pilgrimage, inclusive rejoicing, festival gifts, local judges, impartial justice, bribery, justice pursuit, Asherah, and pillar prohibitions.

verses.restored: Guard month of Aviv and make Passover to YHWH your Divine, for in month of Aviv YHWH your Divine brought you out from Egypt by night.
You shall sacrifice Passover to YHWH your Divine from flock and herd, in place YHWH chooses to cause his name to dwell.

You shall eat no leavened bread with it; seven days you shall eat unleavened bread, bread of affliction, for in haste you came out from land of Egypt, so you may remember day of your coming out from land of Egypt all days of your life.
No leaven shall be seen with you in all your territory seven days, and none of flesh you sacrifice in evening on first day shall remain overnight until morning.
You may not sacrifice Passover within any of your gates YHWH your Divine gives you,
but at place YHWH your Divine chooses to cause his name to dwell, there you shall sacrifice Passover in evening at going down of sun, appointed time of your coming out from Egypt.
You shall cook and eat it in place YHWH your Divine chooses; in morning you shall turn and go to your tents.
Six days you shall eat unleavened bread, and on seventh day solemn assembly to YHWH your Divine; you shall do no work.
Seven weeks you shall count for yourself; from beginning of sickle in standing grain begin to count seven weeks.
You shall make Festival of Weeks to YHWH your Divine with measure of freewill gift of your hand that you give, according to how YHWH your Divine blesses you.

You shall rejoice before YHWH your Divine—you, your son, daughter, male servant, female servant, Levite within your gates, and sojourner, orphan, and widow in your midst—at place YHWH your Divine chooses to cause his name to dwell.
You shall remember you were a slave in Egypt and keep and do these statutes.
Festival of Booths you shall make for yourself seven days, when you gather from threshing floor and winepress.
You shall rejoice in your festival—you, your son, daughter, male servant, female servant, Levite, sojourner, orphan, and widow within your gates.
Seven days you shall celebrate to YHWH your Divine in place YHWH chooses, for YHWH your Divine will bless you in all your produce and all work of your hands, and you shall be altogether joyful.

Three times in year every male of yours shall appear before YHWH your Divine in place he chooses: at Festival of Unleavened Bread, Festival of Weeks, and Festival of Booths; and he shall not appear before YHWH empty.
Each man according to gift of his hand, according to blessing YHWH your Divine gave you.
Judges and officers you shall appoint for yourself in all your gates YHWH your Divine gives you, tribe by tribe; they shall judge people with righteous judgment.
You shall not bend judgment; you shall not recognize faces/show partiality, and you shall not take bribe, for bribe blinds eyes of wise and twists words of righteous.
Justice, justice you shall pursue, so you may live and possess land YHWH your Divine gives you.
You shall not plant for yourself an Asherah of any tree beside altar of YHWH your Divine that you make for yourself.
You shall not raise for yourself a pillar, which YHWH your Divine hates.

translation_notes: The three pilgrimage festivals remain living Jewish sacred times and must not be superseded or detached from Jewish practice.
Passover memory centers hurried liberation and bread of affliction.
Festival joy repeatedly includes servants, Levites, immigrants, orphans, and widows; sacred celebration is socially incomplete without vulnerable neighbors.
Festival giving is proportionate to blessing rather than a flat burden.
Judges are local, tribal, and bound by impartiality and anti-bribery law.
'Justice, justice you shall pursue' makes justice active, repeated, and life-directed.
Asherah and pillar restrictions are ancient Israelite cult law and cannot justify modern destruction of other religions' sacred objects.
safety_note: Do not use Deuteronomy 16 to appropriate Jewish festivals, exclude vulnerable people from religious celebration, corrupt courts through wealth or status, or destroy another community's sacred symbols. Center liberation memory, inclusive joy, proportionate giving, and relentless pursuit of justice.
